title: Bayesian phase transition for the critical Ising model: Enlarged replica symmetry in the epsilon expansion and in 2D
authors: Kay Joerg Wiese, Alapan Das, Adam Nahum
abstract: A process that images or measures bond energies in the critical Ising model can be in distinct measurement ``phases'', depending on the precision of measurement. We study the transition into the strong-measurement phase using replica field theory (an epsilon expansion around six dimensions) and numerical simulations in two dimensions. The results reveal multiscaling of correlation functions at the critical point, and a striking enlarged symmetry of the replica description. This is an analog of the Nishimori phenomenon in the Ising spin glass, in a distinct replica limit. The enlarged symmetry is present microscopically for certain measurement protocols, but more generally can emerge in the infrared, and it fixes the exact value of the exponent for the Edwards-Anderson correlator both in 2D and near the upper critical dimension. We also examine the epsilon expansion for models with power-law interactions and/or long-range measurement.
summary: This paper explores how the precision of measuring bond energies in a critical Ising model can trigger a phase transition between a weak-measurement and a strong-measurement phase. Using both high-dimensional epsilon expansions and 2D numerical simulations, the authors reveal that an enlarged replica symmetry emerges in the infrared limit. This symmetry is powerful enough to fix certain critical exponents exactly. The findings also demonstrate that the system exhibits multiscaling behavior at the critical point.
main_problem: Investigating the Bayesian phase transition in the critical Ising model, where the precision of local measurements drives a transition between weak and strong measurement phases.
main_result: The study identifies an emergent enlarged replica symmetry (G_n+) in the infrared that fixes the exact value of the Edwards-Anderson correlator exponent and demonstrates the existence of multiscaling at the critical point.
method: The authors use epsilon expansion around the upper critical dimension (d=6) at two-loop order, non-perturbative renormalization group (NPRG) analysis, and 2D Monte Carlo simulations.
model_or_system: The system is the critical Ising model subject to noisy measurements of bond energies (Gaussian or binary protocols), described by a replica field theory with spin and overlap fields.
key_observables: Edwards-Anderson correlator, conditioned correlation functions, multiscaling exponents, and the overlap matrix.
important_parameters: Measurement precision (Gamma), spatial dimensionality (d), number of replicas (n), and the coupling ratio (J/Gamma).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the existence of a stable renormalization group fixed point and focuses on the n -> 1 replica limit.
figures_summary: Figures include the coordinates of the fixed point in the epsilon-expansion plane, NPRG eigenvalues and couplings, and finite-size scaling estimates of multiscaling exponents.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Bayesian measurement problem, develops the replica field theory and symmetry analysis, performs epsilon expansion and NPRG calculations, presents 2D numerical simulation results, and discusses long-range interaction extensions.
why_interesting: The emergence of enlarged symmetry from measurement-induced disorder and the study of how information extraction affects critical properties are highly relevant to understanding the impact of monitoring on many-body dynamics and quantum phase transitions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic illustration of part of a critical Ising configura- tion (Left) and the measurement data (Right), which can be thought of as a noisy image of the domain wall configuration. See Fig. 2 for a more accurate depiction of one of the specific lattice measurement protocols discussed in the main text. (Figure from [3].)

Figure 2. A snapshot of the process in an 11 × 11 lattice with pe- riodic boundary conditions. Top left: We start with a configuration S1 = Sref of the pure critical Ising model (blue sites are spin up, red ones spin down). Top right: A collection M of bond measure- ments is made, with normally-distributed errors (the Gaussian pro- tocol). Bottom left: A spin configuration S2 may now be sampled from the a posteriori measure P(•|M). Bottom right: The mea- surement outcomes M define a conditioned two-point correlation G(0, x) = ⟨S(0)S(x)⟩M. The origin 0 is indicated by the black circle. (These snapshots were obtained at J = Jc, Γ = Γc, but no claim is made for their typicality.)

Figure 3. Topology of the phase diagram for the field theory in the (r1, r2) plane (Sec. III A) and also for the lattice model in the (Tc −T, Γ −Γc) plane. The vertical axis, where T = Tc, corre- sponds to the one-pameter phase diagram in (6). Our main focus will be the point at the origin, marked with a circle, where the measure- ment strength is also critical. In the field theory, the diagonal line r1 = r2 possesses an enlarged symmetry (20) if the cubic couplings also respect the enlarged symmetry.
Figure 5. Flow at 2-loop order for n = 1, ϵ = 1. Compared to Fig. 4, a non-trivial fixed point has appeared at λ1 = λ2 = λ∗, see Eq. (48).
Figure 4. Flow diagram at 1-loop order for n = 1, ϵ = 1.

Figure 6. Top: Modified Binder cumulant for the Gaussian measure- ment protocol. The crossing is at Γc ≈0.22 (cf. Eq. 112). Middle: For the binary measurement protocol. The crossing is at Γc ≈0.68. Bottom: For the Gaussian protocol on a checkerboard lattice, where only the diagonal bonds are measured. In the first two cases, we get a good scaling collapse with exponent 1/νM ≈0.39. (The data for the checkerboard case is consistent with a wide range of νM values so we do not show a collapse.)
Figure 8. Log-log plot of the first six moments of the correlator and of the typical value (Eq. 109). The fits giving the exponents ηk, k = 1, ..., 6 for the moments and η0 for the typical are shown.

Figure 7. The quantity R shown for Top: Gaussian measurement, Middle: Binary measurement and Bottom: Gaussian measurement in the checkerboard lattice. The error bars are smaller than the markers. The horizontal dashed line in the first two figures indicates R = 1.

Figure 9. Regions of the (αJ/d, αM/d) plane are labelled according to which cubic terms are relevant. The region that touches the origin is the “mean-field” region where all interactions are irrelevant. In the shaded region a quartic operator becomes relevant (see Sec. IV), in- validating the naive ϵ expansion (unless its coupling is tuned away). The dashed line indicates αM = αJ, in which case there is the pos- sibility of an enlarged symmetry relating ϕ and Φ.
Figure 10. The range where ϵ(α, d) > 0 (in yellow) and two possible expansion paths to reach α = d = 1.
